title: Iran Targets Undersea Cables as Digital Chokepoint, China's Rare Earth Squeeze Persists
subhead: Iran's threat to charge fees for undersea internet cables through the Strait of Hormuz adds a new layer to infrastructure vulnerability, while Japan and the US continue feeling the effects of China's rare earth export controls despite a nominal trade truce.

sections.heading: Iran Claims Authority Over Hormuz Undersea Cables
sections.body:
Iran's military and Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps spokesperson Ebrahim Zolfaghari announced on May 9 that Iran would impose fees on internet cables passing through the Strait of Hormuz. Iranian state media outlets Tasnim and Fars detailed proposals to charge license fees to Meta, Google, Amazon, and Microsoft for cable usage, while claiming Iran alone has the right to repair and maintain subsea cables in the area.

More than 99 percent of international internet traffic runs through undersea cables. The major active cables through the Strait of Hormuz—Asia Africa Europe-1, FALCON, and Gulf Bridge International—primarily serve Gulf countries. The FALCON and Gulf Bridge cables run through Iranian territorial waters at certain points, according to TeleGeography research director Alan Mauldin.

The threat comes amid the ongoing US-Iran conflict. Since February 28, the region has been a no-go zone for cable repair ships. In March, French state-owned Alcatel Submarine Networks suspended a Meta-backed cable project after one of its ships became stranded near Saudi Arabia. Maritime intelligence company Windward noted that operators face a choice: 'pay protection fees and accept Iranian licensing over Middle East Gulf seabed activity, or accept that future faults may go unrepaired indefinitely.'
sections.whyItMatters: Iran is testing a new form of infrastructure leverage—charging transit fees for digital infrastructure in contested waters. This could accelerate efforts by Big Tech and Gulf countries to find alternative routes bypassing the Strait, permanently reshaping Middle East internet topology.

sections.heading: China's Rare Earth Exports to Japan Partially Recover
sections.body:
China's exports of rare earth permanent magnets to Japan recovered only modestly in April after a sharp decline in March, with Japanese firms warning of 'severe' shortages. Exports of yttrium, dysprosium, and terbium remain roughly 50% below pre-control levels compared to the 12 months preceding China's April 2025 export restrictions, according to customs data.

The supply disruption reflects the broader pattern since China imposed export restrictions on heavy rare earths and permanent magnets in April 2025. Despite a one-year trade truce reached between Trump and Xi in October 2025, export flows have been volatile and uneven. European imports jumped 60% year-over-year in November, while US imports fell 11% during the same period.

A CSIS analysis notes that China exported just 17 tons of yttrium to the United States between April and December 2025, compared to 333 tons in the eight months prior to restrictions. Aerospace manufacturers using yttrium as a thermal coating for jet engines have reported rationing and potential production pauses if exports don't increase.
sections.whyItMatters: The trade truce exists on paper but not in practice—China is applying export controls unevenly, favoring some trade partners over others. Japan and the US are learning that Beijing can weaponize supply chain dependencies even during a nominal de-escalation.

sections.heading: US-China Summit Sidelines Semiconductor Controls
sections.body:
The Trump-Xi summit in Beijing concluded on May 15 without substantive agreement on AI or semiconductor governance. Despite Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang joining Trump's delegation at the last minute to secure sales of the H200 chip to China, the talks focused primarily on trade—the 'three Bs' of Boeing, beef, and beans—rather than technology controls.

US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer told Bloomberg that semiconductor export controls were 'not a major topic' in discussions. The White House announced that China would address US concerns about rare earth shortages as part of agreements struck at the summit, though it stopped short of calling for the removal of restrictions that have disrupted supply chains.

The summit took place against the backdrop of the US-Iran war and its impact on chip supply chains. Qatar's LNG facilities at Raas Laffan and Mesaieed halted operations in early March, affecting nearly a third of global helium supply. Helium and other petrochemical byproducts are essential for chipmaking, creating cost pressures for TSMC—which imports over 90% of Taiwan's energy needs, with 40% of LNG coming from the Middle East.
sections.whyItMatters: The summit's focus on agricultural exports over technology suggests both sides are managing tensions rather than resolving them. The real constraint on chips may now be energy supply chain disruption from the Hormuz crisis, not just export controls.

sections.heading: China's Semiconductor Localization Drive Accelerates
sections.body:
A CSIS analysis finds that US and allied export controls on advanced semiconductor technologies, imposed beginning in 2022, have accelerated China's long-standing drive for semiconductor self-reliance. While controls have disrupted Chinese access to leading-edge chips and equipment, their principal effect has been to drive substitution of foreign chips and equipment with domestic alternatives.

Chinese authorities have reportedly directed domestic enterprises to acquire domestic devices and SME that are less advanced than foreign alternatives to advance national self-sufficiency. Since 2022-2023, Chinese end-users have increased sourcing of domestically made chips, reflected in declining sales of foreign chips in China.

China's integrated circuit output fell 9.8% in 2022—the first decline in several years—after export controls were imposed. But the controls have coordinated customer demand within China with the roadmaps of Chinese technology providers. $150 billion in public funding has been channeled into semiconductor localization efforts since 2014-2015, though domestic production accounted for only about 30% of domestic consumption in 2025—short of the 70% target.
sections.whyItMatters: Export controls are working differently than intended: rather than halting China's chip development, they're accelerating domestic substitution and creating a unified industrial push for semiconductor autonomy. The question is whether this leads to a permanently bifurcated semiconductor ecosystem.

sections.heading: Russia-Linked Cyberattacks Target European Infrastructure
sections.body:
UK National Cyber Security Centre head Richard Horne warned that the UK faces 'nationally significant' cyber incidents at a rate of about four per week. The most serious threats come from Russia, Iran, and China, Horne said at the CyberUK conference in Glasgow. The NCSC handled more than 200 incidents last year—more than double the previous year.

China's intelligence agencies display an 'eye-watering level of sophistication' in cyber operations, Horne said, while Iran is 'almost certainly using cyber activity to support the repression of British individuals on our streets.' Russia is applying tactics honed in Ukraine 'beyond the battlefield' against UK and European targets.

Swedish authorities confirmed a pro-Russian group linked to Russian intelligence was behind a cyberattack on a heating plant last year. Poland attributed coordinated attacks on heat and power plants supplying 500,000 customers to hackers 'directly linked to Russian services.' Norway and Denmark also reported infrastructure attacks linked to Russia. The AP has tracked more than 155 incidents of disruption—arson, sabotage, espionage—linked to Russia or its proxies since February 2022.
sections.whyItMatters: European infrastructure is under sustained hybrid attack. The pattern—targeting heating plants, water utilities, and energy systems—shows Russia testing vulnerabilities in critical civilian infrastructure. These are not theoretical risks but ongoing campaigns.

sections.heading: Megaconstellation Pollution Could Reach 42% of Space Sector Impact by 2030
sections.body:
'Megaconstellation' satellite systems like SpaceX's Starlink and China's Qianfan project will be responsible for nearly half of all air pollution from the space sector by 2030, according to University College London researchers. The finding highlights an overlooked environmental cost of the low-earth orbit satellite boom.

SpaceX has over 18,000 satellites in orbit, with more than half belonging to megaconstellations. The company has applied for permission to launch up to 1 million additional Starlink satellites. In less than a decade, the number of satellites has more than doubled.

The pollution comes from launch emissions and satellite reentry debris accumulating in the upper atmosphere. Scientists have warned this creates a climate effect 'a little bit like geoengineering'—an untested experiment on atmospheric chemistry. A recent SpaceX satellite malfunction and breakup over Earth highlighted concerns about orbital debris as megaconstellation density increases.
sections.whyItMatters: The environmental governance of space is lagging behind launch activity. As Starlink and competitors scale toward hundreds of thousands of satellites, atmospheric pollution and orbital debris are becoming systemic risks—not just for astronomy, but for climate and space operations.

bottomLine: Today's tech-power landscape shows infrastructure weaponization from multiple directions: Iran threatening undersea cables, Russia conducting cyberattacks on European utilities, and China applying selective pressure on rare earth exports despite a trade truce. The Trump-Xi summit produced agricultural deals but no resolution on semiconductor controls, while China's domestic chip substitution accelerates in response to US restrictions. The throughline is that control points—whether undersea chokepoints, mineral supply chains, or satellite orbits—are being contested more aggressively, and the rules governing them are being rewritten in real time.
sources: Ars Technica, AP News, CSIS, TIME, The Diplomat, Bloomberg, Reuters, TechRepublic, E&T, The Guardian, CNN, Windward, TeleGeography
